questions.prompt: Why is React Hook Form + Zod the modern pattern over raw controlled inputs with useState?

choices.text: RHF uses uncontrolled inputs (refs-based) so each keystroke doesn't re-render the entire form — for forms with 20+ fields, this is dramatically faster. Zod centralizes validation in one schema; `z.infer<typeof schema>` derives the form's TypeScript types so they stay in sync automatically. Same schema can run server-side (Server Actions, API routes), so client and server use one source of truth for validation. Less boilerplate than manual onChange/value plumbing, fewer re-renders, type safety end-to-end. The 2026 default for React forms.

questions.explanation: Fewer re-renders + schema-derived types + shared client/server validation.

questions.prompt: Form has 30 fields. Performance feels sluggish on each keystroke. Diagnostic and fix?

choices.text: Likely fully-controlled with useState — each keystroke triggers a full form re-render, all 30 fields go through render. Switch to React Hook Form: inputs become uncontrolled (ref-based), only individual fields re-render on change, the form root only re-renders when needed. Also check for unnecessary `watch()` calls — every watch triggers re-renders on its field changes; use sparingly (only when one field's value gates another's display). For most cases the move from controlled-useState to RHF is a 5-10x perf improvement on form interactions.

questions.explanation: Controlled state per field re-renders the whole form per keystroke. RHF's ref-based approach fixes it.
